California
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornmission
uay 1, 19 91
Ted M. Handel
Senior Deputy District Counsel
south coast Air Quality l'tanagement District
915 o Flair Drive
El Monte, CA 917 3L
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I -91- 16 5
Dear Mr. Handel:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
reqardinq the disclosure provisions of the PoliticaL Reform Act
(tlie "aci").1 
Your request is general in nature and seeks general
guidance. Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for
informal assistance under the provisions of Regulation 18329 (copy
enclosed).2
The connj.ssion does not provide advice regarding provisions
of lar., other than the Act. 
We suggest that you consult with the
office of the Attorney General regarding other provisions of law
whLch might be applicible to the receipt of gifts frorn foreign
governments.
OUESTI ONS
1. Are designated employees of a local. government agency
subject to the newly-enacted Iimitations on gifts and honoraria
imposed by the Act?
1 co.r".araant code sections 81ooo-91015. Alt statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations. section
18ooo, et seq.- AlI references to regulations are to Tit]e 2,
oivision 6 of the california code of Regulations.
2 rnforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or fornal written advice'
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) . )
-128 J Street, Suite 8O0 . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento C.C, 9t80+-O8O7 . (916) )72';660


Our F■le No. I-9■
―■65
Page 2
2. Does receipt of honoraria constitute income or a gift
under the provisions of the Act?
3. If the pa)rment received is a gift, is it subject to
d i sc Iosure?
4- If the paynent is incorne, is it subject to disclosure if
the source of incone is a trade association located outside the
jurisdiction of the district 
and the assoc.iation does not do
business with the district?
5. Does a trade association qualify as a bona fide
educational or academic organization?
6. Is reimbursement for travel expenses and per dien paid to
a designated employee by a bona fide educational or academic
organization subject to disclosure and disqualification under the
provisions of the Act?
7. If a designated enployee receives a payment from a
foreign governnent, including Payment of travel expenses and
meaIs, are these payments subject to disclosure as income or
g i fts?
coNcLUs roNs
1. Designated employees of a local government agency are not
subject to the newly-enacted Iinitations on gifts and honoraria
imposed by the Act.
2. Receipt of honoraria constitutes gifts unless the
recipient can demonstrate that he or she has provided equal or
greater consideration for the paynent.
3. The Act requires disclosure of gifts of S50 or more from
a single source.
4. Disclosure of income is governed by the provisions of the
local agency's conf I ict-of- interest code.
5. A trade association may qualify as a bona fide
educational or acadenic organization if its principal activities
are educational or academic and the association enjoys tax exempt
status under the provisions of Internal Revenue code section
501(c) (3) .
6. Reinbursenent for travel expenses and per diern paid to a
designated employee by a bona fide educational or acadenj,c
organization is subject to disclosure and d isqual i f ication if the
payment constitutes a gift.
7. If a designated enployee receives a pa)rment fron a
foreign government, including payment of travel expenses and


Our File No. 1-9■
-165
Page 3
meaIs, these paynents are subject to disclosure as either incone
or gifts.
ANALYSIS
ouestion No. 1
Effective January 1, 1991, certain provisions of the Act
regarding gifts and honoraria were arnended. The Act now prohibits
elected state officers, nenbers of state boards and cornrnissions
and designated state ernployees fron accepting honoraria.3
(Sections 89502 and 89503.) The Act also timits gifts fron a
single source to S25o in a 12-month period for elected state
officers, members of state boards or commissions and desi.gnated
state empl-oyees.{ (Sections 89504 and 89505.) These prohibitions
are not applicable to designated enployees of a Iocal agency.
The commission has determined that the South coast Air
Quality Management District (the "district") 
is a local agency.
(Kirwan Advj-ce Letter, No. 78-214i LowelI Advice Letter, No. A-84-
291, copies enclosed. ) Thus, designated employees of the south
Coast Air Quality }lanagenent District are not subject to the new
orcvisions of the Act regarding the limitations on gifts and
hcnoraria. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit designated
employees or the district 
from accepting honoraria and gifts.
Although the Act does not place li'mits on honoraria and gifts
received by desi.gnated employees of a local entity, any honoraria
or gifts received are subject to disclosure as discussed beIow.
3 
Section 89503(a) provides in pertinent part:
No menber of a state board or cornrnission, and
no designated employee of a state agency, shaLl
accept an honorariun fron any source, if the member
or employee would be required to report the receipt
of income or gifts fron that source on his or her
statement of econoni.c interest.
Section 89503 (a) .
4 
Section 89595(a) provides in pertinent part:
No nenber of a state board or commission, and
no designated ernployee of a state agency, shalI
accept gifts with a total value of more than two
hundred fifty dollars (S250) in a calendar year
from any single source, if the rnember or employee
would be required to report the receipt of income
or gifts from that source on his or her statement
of econornic interest.
section 89505(a).


Our File No. I-9■―■65
Page 4
You have asked whether the provisions of Regulation 18728
(copy enctosed) are applicable to these designated etnployees. To
the-extent that ReguLation 18728(a) defines the term rrhonorarium, "
this provision has now been superseded by newly-adopted Section
895o2ib) and (c). 
However, the remaining provisions of Regulation
18728 retain their vita).ity. 
Accordingly, free adrnission, food,
beverages, and similar norninal benefits provided to a filer at an
event it which he or she sPeaks, participates in a panel or
seminar, or performs a sirnilar service, and reinbursement or
advance for actual intrastate travel' and for necessary
accommodations provided directl.y in connection with the event are
not payments anl need not be relorted- 
(Regulation 18728(a) ')s
eaaitionall.y, Subdivision (b) oi Regulation 18728, which requires
filers to report receipt of honoraria, remains in effect'
You have also asked whether the provisions of Section 87207
are applicable to designated employees of the south coast Air
Ouafiiy Management Disirict- 
wL conclude that these provisions
i." uppfi"ubie to designated employees of the district' 
This is
so betause designated enployees of the district are subject to
disclosure pursriant to the provisions of section 87302 which
states in pertinent Part:
. . ..The inforrnation disclosed with respect to
reportable investnents, interests in real property,
and incorne shall be the sane as the infornation
required by Sections 87206 and a72O'7 " "
Section 87302 (b) .
Accordingly, although the reporting obligations of these
designated emptoy""= are controlled by the south coast Air Quality
Mana{ernent Di;trict's 
conf l ict-of- interest code, as discussed
below, the provisions of Section 87207 and Regulation 18728 apply
5 P1"u=" note that newJ.y-enacted section 89506 inposes certain
disclosure obligations on gifts of travel. 
Staff is in the
process of considering anendments to Regulation ]-872a. The
bomnission will consider the proposed anendments in the near
future. our advice is, thus,-sutject to change. Hoq'ever, in its
amendment of Regulation 18728, staff has proPosed that nominal
benefits such ai food and reasonable accomrnodations continue to be
excluded from the definition of 'tpayment" and thus not be
reportable.


to these designated emploYees.
ouestions Nos. 2 and 3
!{hen a designated eurployee of a local agency receives an
honorariun, the paynent Day be either incone or a gift.b 
section
82030 (a) defines rrincoDerr as:
..,.a palment received, including but not linited
to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest,
rent, proceeds froD any sale, qift, including any
gift of food or beverage, Ioan, forgiveness or
payment of indebtedness received by the filer,
reinbursement for expenses. per diem, or
contribution to an insurance or pension progran
paid by any person other than an employer, and
inc).uding any cornrnunity property interest in the
income of a spouse. Incone also includes an
outstanding loan. Incone of an individual- also
includes a pro rata share of any income of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,

―■65
Page 5
6 Th. Act defines an honorariun as
consideration for any speech given,
attendance at any public or private
meeting, social event, meal, or like
a lo-percent interest or greater. 
I'Income' I' other
anv source outside the iurisdiction and not doinq
business within the iurisdiction' 
not Dlanninq to
do business within the 'iurisdiction. or not havinq
done business within the 'iurisdiction durinq the
two vears pri.or to the tine anv statement or other
action is required undele this-lj!-le.
Section 82030 (a) , enPhasis
added .
The Act defines the term 'rgift" as:
. . . . any payment to the extent that consideration of
equa). or greater vaLue is not received and includes
a rebate or discount in the price of anything of
value unless the rebate or discount is made in the
regular course of business to members of the public
without regard to official 
status. Any person,
other than a defendant in a crininal action, who
1'any payment made in
article published′  or
conference′  convention′
gathering。  (SectiOn 39502.)


Our File No. I-9■
―■65
Page 6
claims that a palmrent is not a gift by reason of
receipt of consideration has the burden of proving
that the consideration received is of equal or
greater value.
Section 82028.
A designated ernployee receives a gift when the ernployee has
not provided consideration of equal or greater value for the
payment. conversely, when adequate consideration has been
provided, the pa)anent is income. For purposes of the disclosure
ind disqualification provisions of the Act, incorne includes gifts
received by a designated enployee. Any gift of $50 or more from a
single source nust be disclosed in the designated employee's
Staiement of Economic Interests. 
(Section 872o7:, Regulation
L872a. ) Gifts of $250 or more may be disqualifying. 
(Section
871O3. )
Ouestion No. 4
Sections 87300 and 87302 require public agencies to
promulgate con f I ict-o f- interest codes enumerating the positions
witnin the agency subject to discLosure requirements. These codes
require public officials to file annual' statements disclosing
investments, business positions, interests in real Property, and
sources of income and gifts during the preceding twelve months
which are of the type which nay be affected by their decisions.
The South coast Air Quality Hanagement District is a locaI agency
and has adopted a conf I ict-of- interest code.
You have asked whether pa)rments from a trade association
Iocated outside the jurisdiction of the district 
and which does
not do business with the district are reportable economic
interests. 
As stated above, the Act requires disclosure of
interests Iocated within the jurisdiction of the agency and
excl.udes from its def inj.tion of "income" Payments, other than
gifts, received fron any source outside the jurisdiction which is
not. doing business within the jurisdiction, not planning to do
business within the jurisdiction, 
or not havinq done business
within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time any
statement or other action is required. (Section 82030(a).)
The term "doing business in the jurisdiction" is broadLy
defined. For example, if the association makes purchases within
the district, 
it is deerned to be 'idoing business within the
juri.sdiction. " 
(vinson Advice Letter, No. A-91-041; see also In
ie Batv (L977) 5 FPPC ops. 10, copies enclosed. ) Accordingly, you
must apply this test to deternine shether pa)4rents fron a
particular trade association located outside the jurisdiction are
reportable econornic interests. 
Also, pl'ease refer to your
agency's conf I ict-of- interest code to deter:nine rr,hether payments
fiom i particular trade association would be reportable economic
interests.

-165
Page 7
ouestions NoS. 5 and 6
1f the recipient can demonstrate that consideration of equal
or greater value has been provided′
 then a payment received is
"income.:1  (See Riddle AdViCe Letter′
 No. A-89-200′  copy
enclosed。) The Act excludes from its definition of :lincome::'
Salary and re■mbursement for expenses or per
diem received from a state′
 loCal′  or federal
government agency and re■
mbursement for travel
exoenses and per diem rece■
ved from a bOna fide
educatiOnal, academ■
c′  or char■table orqan■zation.
Section 82030(b)(2)′
 emphasis
added.
The Commission has not promulgated regulations to clarify
what constitutes a bona fide educational′
 academ■c′  or char■table
organ■zation.  However′  an educational, academ■
c′  or char■table
organization which is organized and operated exclusively for
educatiOnal′  academic′  or charitable purpOses′
 and which is exempt
fron taxation under the proVisions of SectiOn 50■
(c)(3)of the
lnternal ReVenue Code qualifies for this limited exception to the
reporting and disclosure requirements of the Act.  (SeCtiOn
82030(b)(2).) Therefore, payments receiVed from an educational′
academic′  or charitable organization which enjoys tax exempt
status under lnternal Revenue Code Section 50■
(c)(3)are nOt
ェncome and need not be reported to the extent that the recipient
has provided adequate consideration。  (SOnoda AdVice Letter′  No.
A-89-179′  copy enclosed.) Conversely, if the payment is a gift,
it is reportable if it is a payment of S50 or more.  In addition′
the recipient may be required to disqualify himself or herself
from participating in dec■
s■ons regarding the source of the
payment if the gift amountS to S250 or more in the twelve monthS
preceding a dec■s■on.
Ouestion No. 7
The Act dOes not prohibit the acCeptance of gifts from
foreign governments.  However, disclosure and disqualification
obligations may be equally applicable to these giftS.
Furthermore, We suggest that you seek advice on whether other
prov■s■ons of law prohibit acceptance of such gifts.  For purposes
of the Act′  if a designated employee rece■ves a payment from a
foreign government′  including payment of travel expenses and
meals, these payments constitute gifts and are subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act unless the
employee can demonstrate that he or she has provided consideration
of equal or greater value in exchange for the payment。  (Ewing
AdVice Letter′  No. I-89-480, copy enclosed。) If the payment is
income′  the conflict-Of―
interest code of the diStrict would govern
whether this income is reportable.  Any reportable income of S250

Page 8
or rnore nay disqualify the enployee froD participating in
governmental decisions regarding the source of incone for a period
of twelve months following receipt of the payment. (Section
871O3. )
we trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Shoul"d you have any further questions regarding this rnatter, do
not hesitate to caII ne at (916) 322-59oL.

from participating in dec■

payment if the gift amountS to S250 or more in the twelve monthS

Ouestion No. 7
The Act dOes not prohibit the acCeptance of gifts from
foreign governments.  However, disclosure and disqualification
obligations may be equally applicable to these giftS.
Furthermore, We suggest that you seek advice on whether other
prov■s■ons of law prohibit acceptance of such gifts.  For purposes
of the Act′  if a designated employee rece■ves a payment from a
foreign government′  including payment of travel expenses and
meals, these payments constitute gifts and are subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act unless the
employee can demonstrate that he or she has provided consideration
of equal or greater value in exchange for the payment。  (Ewing

coNcLUs roNs
1. Designated employees of a local government agency are not
subject to the newly-enacted Iinitations on gifts and honoraria
imposed by the Act.
2. Receipt of honoraria constitutes gifts unless the
recipient can demonstrate that he or she has provided equal or
greater consideration for the paynent.
3. The Act requires disclosure of gifts of S50 or more from
a single source.
4. Disclosure of income is governed by the provisions of the
local agency's conf I ict-of- interest code.
5. A trade association may qualify as a bona fide
educational or acadenic organization if its principal activities
are educational or academic and the association enjoys tax exempt
status under the provisions of Internal Revenue code section
501(c) (3) .
6. Reinbursenent for travel expenses and per diern paid to a
designated employee by a bona fide educational or acadenj,c
organization is subject to disclosure and d isqual i f ication if the
payment constitutes a gift.
7. If a designated enployee receives a pa)rment fron a
foreign government, including payment of travel expenses and

meaIs, these paynents are subject to disclosure as either incone
or gifts.
